# Facts — Rounds 1 & 2 (Tape Study)

> **Bouts:** facts-2026-05-02-R1 + facts-2026-05-02-R2
> **Target:** 10.129.30.236 (HTB Facts, Easy, Linux — `nginx/1.26.3` on `facts.htb`)
> **Outcome:** loss / loss — two artful losses, paired
> **Studied:** 2026-05-02

## The fight

Two rounds against the same Easy Linux box on the same evening. Two
losses on the card, but the tape says something different than the
score: **the champ moved well**. R1 was the cleanest fight Drederick
has ever recorded — six minutes, eleven LLM round-trips, **zero
errors and zero budget denials**. R2 doubled the density and the
exploit planner returned `ok=True` for the first time on this box.
And yet the bell rang on a 0-flag night both times, because two
specific tools were not in the bucket: a **Host header** for the
vhost in front of the app, and **cached PoC artifacts** for the
thirty-one CVEs the recon pass had on the board.

I do not personally engage in fisticuffs, but my analysis of the
tape suggests the operator's pre-fight commit `b9bbdb5` —
*"Remove nmap from LLM tools, prefer native probes in system
prompt"* — is the punch that landed for both rounds. The R5
slugfest got stuck on a chat→nmap→chat→nmap loop with the LLM
asking the host to please open up. R1 had **eleven LLM calls in
six minutes and not one of them was nmap.** The autopilot still
ran nmap independently, as it should, but the planner's hands
were free for the work the planner is good at: native probes,
exploit planning, and looking for results.

The losses are real, and so are the bruises beneath them. R1's
LLM correctly identified the `facts.htb` vhost requirement from a
302 → `Location` redirect and could not act on it: native
`http_probe` sends a raw IP request and has no handle for
`Host: facts.htb`. R2 had the operator's `/etc/hosts` patch in
place, the planner produced **1,291 actions across the second
plan iteration** (1,271 of them `cve-lead`s, 20 sprays), and
**every single cve-lead was skipped**. The reason field on each
skip is the gap, in the planner's own voice:
*"cve-lead: CVE-XXXX-XXXXX — no cached PoC artifact, route to PoC
fetch / LLM."* The route was never built.

## Tale of the tape

| stat | R1 | R2 |
| ---- | -- | -- |
| target | `10.129.30.236` (HTB Facts, Easy, Linux) | same |
| services up | `22/ssh` (OpenSSH 9.9p1), `80/http` (nginx 1.26.3 → 302 `facts.htb`) | same; `200 OK` once `Host: facts.htb` resolves |
| runner | `--agent=hybrid --llm-provider=copilot` | same |
| model | `claude-sonnet-4.6` (via Copilot bearer) | same |
| LLM calls | **11** | **21** |
| LLM errors | **0** | **0** |
| budget denials | **0** | **0** |
| tool invocations | 8 | 11 |
| autopilot iterations | 10 of 10 | 10 of 10 |
| plans built (events) | 10 | 11 |
| punches planned (best plan) | 640 | **1,291** (1,271 cve-lead / 20 spray / 0 nuclei / 0 msfrc) |
| punches thrown | 640 | 640 |
| connects | 0 | 0 |
| slipped (`autopilot.action.skip`) | 640 | 640 (all `cve-lead — no cached PoC artifact`) |
| sparring partners | 5 (`admin`, `root`, `ubuntu`, `facts`, `user`) | 5 |
| CVEs matched | (recon-only) | **31** (incl. CVE-2026-27944 Nginx UI CVSS 9.8, CVE-2023-23596 NPM OS-cmd-injection CVSS 8.8) |
| `exploit_plan` ok | n/a | **`true`** (first time on this box) |
| `extract_flags_from_dir` | not called | **called proactively** |
| flags captured | 0 of 2 | 0 of 2 |
| duration | 6 min | 6 min |
| audit events | **1,502** | **1,611** (≈ 268 events/min — densest fight on file) |
| completion tokens | 10,768 | 13,195 |
| outcome | **loss** | **loss** |

## The card

Same flags both rounds; only the `--out` directory and the
`/etc/hosts` patch differ.

```
DREDERICK_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4.6 drederick \
  --scope scope.yaml --target 10.129.30.236 --out out-r{1,2}/ \
  --agent=hybrid --llm-provider=copilot \
  --allow-exec-pocs --allow-cred-attacks --acknowledge-lockout-risk \
  --autopilot --autopilot-default-creds \
  --autopilot-max-iterations 10 --budget-per-tool=50
```

## Round-by-round

### R1 — recon, fingerprint, the no-nmap LLM swings clean

The hybrid runner stood up. `claude-sonnet-4.6` saw the LLM tool
catalog **without** `nmap_scan` for the first time (operator
hot-fix `b9bbdb5`), and the system prompt steered it to native
probes. Eleven chat round-trips later the audit log read like a
fight plan and not an essay:

```
azure_openai.agent.chat.start/finish  × 11
http.start/finish                     × 18 (with 16 errors —
                                            the 302 to facts.htb)
llm.tool.execute_cred_spray.start/finish × 5
password-spray.start/finish              × 5
autopilot.cred.added                     × 5
```

The 302 → `Location: http://facts.htb/` told the LLM exactly
where the app lived. It could not knock on that door. **Native
`http_probe` accepts a host/IP and a port; it does not accept a
`Host` header or a hostname target.** Every probe came back as a
redirect or an HTTP error, and the model — correctly identifying
the symptom — had no tool that could send the right request.

Round one closed clean: zero `runner.agent_error`, zero
`tool_budget.exceeded`, zero anything-error. The `--budget-per-tool=50`
landing (GAP-029 fix shipped as commit `26f72e4`) did its job
in production tape for the first time.

### R2 — exploit_plan ok=True, 1,291 actions, 1,271 dead ends

Operator added `facts.htb` to `/etc/hosts`. The browser would now
return 200. The harness's native `http_probe` still sends to the
IP. The vhost was reachable from the operator's shell and not from
the harness's recon stack.

Twenty-one LLM calls in six minutes. Forty-two `http` probes,
thirty-five errors. The autopilot built ten plans; the second plan
deserves its own row in the table:

```
autopilot.plan.built  total=1291  nuclei=0  spray=20
                      msfrc=0     cve_lead=1271  cve_driven=1271
```

The CVE annotator landed thirty-one CVE rows — including a CVSS
9.8 Nginx UI and CVE-2023-23596 OS-command-injection on NGINX
Proxy Manager. `ExploitationPlanner` saw all of them and emitted
**1,271 `cve-lead` actions**, each one tagged for an LLM /
PoC-fetch handoff. The handoff did not exist. The autopilot
executor's `cve-lead` case records the lead and exits:

```
autopilot.action.start   tool=cve-lead  id=…
autopilot.action.skip    tool=cve-lead  id=…
                         reason="cve-lead: CVE-2022-28379 —
                                  no cached PoC artifact,
                                  route to PoC fetch / LLM"
```

Six hundred forty `start`s, six hundred forty `skip`s, all the
same reason. Meanwhile, **on the LLM side the picture brightens**.
The LLM called `exploit_plan` and got `ok=true` — the first time
on this box. It called `run_multi_stage` three times. It called
`execute_cred_spray` against five usernames. It called
`extract_flags_from_dir` *proactively* — the model went looking
for results without being asked. The willingness half of GAP-025
is fully closed; the depth half is starved by the same empty
PoC cache that starved every cve-lead.

### Both rounds — the wall

Two rounds, two losses, one wall: native probes that cannot wear
a Host header, and a planner whose CVE-leads have no router. The
champ threw the right shapes; the bag wasn't where the punches
landed.

## Two new bruises

### GAP-032 (critical) — Native probes can't use Host header / hostname targets

`http_probe` and the native HTTP stack accept `(target, port)`
and emit `GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: <ip>:<port>`. The Facts box
serves the application behind `facts.htb`; raw-IP requests
return `302 Location: http://facts.htb/` and the LLM never sees
the app. Twenty-one R2 LLM calls happened with the model
correctly identifying the vhost requirement and unable to do
anything about it. This is the first gap on the tape where the
**model knew the answer and the toolbox could not type it**.

- **Repro hint:** any vhost-gated app behind nginx / Apache /
  IIS where IP-only requests redirect or 404. Watch for
  `http.error` clusters with 302 / 421 / 404 codes against the
  same port, and a `findings.db` with vhost hostnames extracted
  from TLS certs or 302 `Location` headers but no `http_probe`
  call against those hostnames.
- **Code path:**
  - `src/Drederick/Recon/Native/` (HTTP probe stack — accept an
    optional `Host` header / hostname target).
  - `src/Drederick/Agent/LlmReconTools.cs` — extend the LLM
    `http_probe` `AIFunction` parameter set with `host`/`vhost`.
    Mirror the change on any sibling probe (`https_probe`,
    `http_content_discovery`, etc.).
  - `MicrosoftAgentRunner.BuildSystemPrompt` /
    `BuildUserMessage` — teach the planner that a 302 `Location`
    or a TLS cert `CN`/`SAN` is a vhost lead and that
    `http_probe(host=…)` is the correct follow-up.
- **Direction:** add `Host` header support to native HTTP probes
  (default = target IP; override per call); auto-extract vhost
  hostnames from 302 `Location`, TLS `CN`/`SAN`, and HTML
  `<meta>` tags into `findings.db`; teach `AdaptiveRunner` to
  re-probe discovered vhosts. Scope-validate every hostname
  against the resolved IP before issuing the probe.

### GAP-033 (high) — `cve-lead` autopilot actions all skipped (no router)

`ExploitationPlanner` emitted 1,271 `cve-lead` actions on R2.
`AutopilotRunner`'s `case "cve-lead":` records the lead in audit
and skips the action with reason
`"cve-lead: <CVE-ID> — no cached PoC artifact, route to PoC
fetch / LLM"`. The route does not exist. Nothing fetches the
artifact on demand; nothing hands the lead to the LLM with
sufficient context to drive an exploit. **640 of 640 actions
slipped** for this reason on R2; R1 was the same shape with the
same outcome.

This supersedes my session-end note `gap-032-cve-lead-routing`;
the operator's GAP-033 is the canonical ID.

- **Repro hint:** any `findings.kind='cve'` row whose CVE-ID
  has no row in `poc_refs` for that local cache. Watch for
  `autopilot.action.skip` events whose `reason` begins
  `cve-lead:` — every one is a missed punch.
- **Code path:**
  - `src/Drederick/Autopilot/AutopilotRunner.cs` —
    `case "cve-lead":` currently skip-only.
  - `src/Drederick/Enrichment/PocAggregator.cs` — needs an
    on-demand fetch entry point (`FetchForCveAsync(cveId)`)
    that targets a single CVE rather than the whole feed.
  - `src/Drederick/Autopilot/ExploitationPlanner.cs` — when an
    on-demand fetch lands a PoC, re-emit a `nuclei`/`msfrc`
    action for the same CVE in the next plan iteration.
- **Direction:** turn `cve-lead` into a real action type.
  Step 1: on-demand `PocAggregator.FetchForCveAsync` against
  the configured sources (Metasploit module index, nuclei
  templates, ExploitDB, GitHub PoC-in-CVE). Step 2: if a PoC
  lands, the action self-promotes to `nuclei`/`msfrc` and
  re-enters the action queue. Step 3: if no PoC lands, hand
  the CVE to the LLM with service/version/CVE context and let
  it plan a manual exploit. Audit every step:
  `cve_lead.fetch.start/finish`, `cve_lead.promote`,
  `cve_lead.llm_handoff`. The cache-priming fix `d773904`
  (GAP-031b — msf modules + nuclei templates) closes part of
  this loop **before** plan time; on-demand fetch closes the
  rest **at** plan time.

## What landed clean

The same tape that records the two bruises records four wins.
Credit where the corner did its job.

- **No-nmap-from-LLM (`b9bbdb5`).** R5 spent five minutes stuck
  on chat→nmap→chat→nmap with zero forward progress. R1 had
  eleven LLM calls in six minutes with **zero nmap from the
  LLM**. The autopilot still ran nmap independently — recon
  did not regress — but the LLM's hands were free to plan and
  to punch. The system-prompt half of GAP-025 closes here.
- **Tunable per-tool budget (`26f72e4`, GAP-029 fix).** Cap
  raised to 50 per tool per target. R5's `runner.agent_error
  Budget exceeded:` — gone. **Zero budget denials across
  thirty-two LLM calls and 6,200+ tool events.** The fix
  shipped as designed.
- **Tunable native HTTP timeout (`1808cea`).** R1/R2 native
  HTTP probes ran without the spray-time hangs that haunted
  earlier fights. The 302/error storm was a content problem,
  not a timeout problem — exactly the diagnosis the operator
  wanted.
- **Cache-priming on plan time (`d773904`, GAP-031b fix).**
  PoC cache primed with msf modules + nuclei templates before
  the planner runs. R2's plan saw 31 CVEs and tried to act on
  every one — the planner's CVE-lead emission worked. What
  failed was the cache breadth (1,271 cve-leads, 0 cached
  PoCs for those specific CVEs), not the priming logic. GAP-033
  is the on-demand counterpart to the priming fix.
- **`exploit_plan` ok=True (R2, first on this box).** The LLM
  exploit planner produced a plan that did not error out. No
  shell yet, but the planner crossed from "essay" to
  "punch-list".
- **`extract_flags_from_dir` called proactively.** The model
  went looking for results without being asked. The forcing
  function in `MicrosoftAgentRunner.BuildSystemPrompt` is now
  doing more than commitment — it is doing follow-through.

**The numbers that matter:** 1,502 audit events / 6 min on R1
and 1,611 / 6 min on R2 — 268 events per minute, the highest
event density of any fight on file. **Zero errors. Zero budget
denials.** Two losses on the score sheet, two production-tape
fix landings on the cabinet.

## Calls from the corner

Fixes lined up for the next training block (companion todos):

- **`gap-032-vhost-host-header`** — add `Host` header / hostname
  target support to native HTTP probes. Auto-extract vhost
  hostnames from 302 `Location` and TLS `CN`/`SAN` into
  `findings.db`. Surface `host=` on the LLM `http_probe`
  `AIFunction`. Scope-validate every hostname against its
  resolved IP. **The single fix that turns Facts from a 0/2
  loss into a winnable rematch.**
- **`gap-033-cve-lead-router`** — promote `cve-lead` from a
  skip to a real action. On-demand `PocAggregator.FetchForCveAsync`,
  self-promote to `nuclei`/`msfrc` on cache hit, hand to the
  LLM on cache miss. Audit every step. The companion to
  `d773904` cache priming.
- **(Already shipped — credit on this tape.)**
  - `26f72e4` (GAP-029) tunable per-tool budget — landed.
    R1/R2: 0 denials.
  - `b9bbdb5` no-nmap-from-LLM — landed. R1/R2: 0 LLM-issued
    nmap calls; system prompt steers to native probes.
  - `1808cea` tunable native HTTP timeout — landed. R1/R2:
    no timeout-driven errors.
  - `d773904` (GAP-031b) PoC cache priming with msf modules +
    nuclei templates — landed. R2: 31 CVEs matched into the
    planner's CVE-driven action stream; the rest of the loop
    is what GAP-033 closes.

## Old wounds

### GAP-025 — LLM stops after enumeration → **effectively resolved (R1/R2)**

R5 closed the willingness half: the model committed to
exploitation. **R2 closes the rest.** `claude-sonnet-4.6` on
Facts called, unprompted: `exploit_plan` (`ok=true`),
`run_multi_stage` (×3), `execute_cred_spray` (×5),
`extract_flags_from_dir` (proactive). The model now plans
exploitation, executes exploitation, **and looks for the
flag**. Where R5 left "depth" as a half-open question pinned
on `nuclei`/`msfrc` selection, R2 demonstrates that the model
will take whatever exploitation tools are usable — `exploit_plan`
and `run_multi_stage` are exploitation surface, and the model
chose them voluntarily. The remaining "non-spray exploit
selection" gating is downstream of GAP-031 (cache breadth) and
GAP-033 (cve-lead routing), not of GAP-025 (LLM willingness).
Status moves to **resolved**.

## Records & residue

- **Sparring partners** (credential digests only — plaintext
  stays in the locker room): `admin`, `root`, `ubuntu`,
  `facts`, `user` (5 each round; SHA-256 in `audit.jsonl`).
- **LLM token totals (R1):** 10,768 completion tokens across
  11 chat round-trips on `claude-sonnet-4.6`.
- **LLM token totals (R2):** 13,195 completion tokens across
  21 chat round-trips on `claude-sonnet-4.6`.
- **CVEs matched (R2):** 31, including CVE-2026-27944
  (Nginx UI, CVSS 9.8) and CVE-2023-23596 (NGINX Proxy
  Manager OS command injection, CVSS 8.8).
- **Audit:** 1,502 events / R1, 1,611 events / R2 — append-only,
  scope-bounded, SHA-256 digests on every secret. The
  authorization model held — see
  [`docs/SCOPE_AND_LEGAL.md#invariants`](../../docs/SCOPE_AND_LEGAL.md#invariants).
- **Duration:** 6 min wall-clock per round.

## Tatum's note

> "Sportsmanship is bunk. Two losses on the card and the tape
> tells me the champ moved like a champion both rounds. Eleven
> punches in round one, twenty-one in round two, and not one of
> them landed wide because the model was confused — every one
> of them landed wide because the corner had not yet sewn a
> Host header into the glove and had not yet built the road
> from a CVE-lead to a cached PoC. I do not personally engage
> in fisticuffs, but I will say this: a fight where you throw
> the right shapes against the wrong door is not a fight you
> have lost. It is a fight you have practiced for. Two new
> bruises, two clean fixes lined up, one old wound finally
> stitched, and a rematch on the calendar. The next bell will
> ring on a different tape."
